calculated_metrics.confidence_note: Cost per outcome calculation is not available. This organisation conducts significant multi-domain work (e.g., human education, environmental conservation). Dividing the total budget solely by quantified animal outcomes would artificially inflate the cost and misrepresent their financial efficiency.

details.formula: Calculation aborted due to multi-domain operations.

calculated_metrics.name: Cost Per Outcome (USD)

source.quote: engaged 2,242 migrant domestic workers through March 2025.

metrics.context_qualifier: Native tree and shrub seedlings of almost 200 species planted in a one-hectare area for forest restoration (July-October 2024).
metrics.metric_name: Native Tree and Shrub Seedlings Planted

source.quote: From July to October 2024, more than 4,000 tree and shrub seedlings representing almost 200 native species-all of them tagged and measured in the nursery beforehand-were then planted into an area of just under one ha.

metrics.context_qualifier: Seeds and propagules collected for plant conservation and raised in nurseries over the past year.

source.quote: Over the past year, they collected over 70,000 seeds and propagules, raised thousands of seedlings and cuttings in our nurseries and accessioned them in our database.

metrics.context_qualifier: Over-abundant pioneer trees thinned to create space for diverse native seedlings as part of forest restoration (April-June 2024).

source.quote: more than 475 over-abundant pioneer trees (primarily Machilus chekiangensis, M. pauhoi and Mallotus paniculatus) had been thinned out

metrics.context_qualifier: New plant species added to the Lower Hillside landscape to enhance ecology over the past year.

source.quote: More than 200 species were added to the Lower Hillside landscape over the year

metrics.context_qualifier: Biochar produced from woody debris using a biochar machine for soil improvement over the year.

source.quote: resulting in the production of 44 tonnes of biochar.

metrics.context_qualifier: Schools participating in a program to deepen understanding of snakes (April 2024 - March 2025).

source.quote: a school programme engaged primary and secondary students across four schools to deepen their understanding of snakes.

metrics.context_qualifier: Schools partnered for habitat creation project for Golden Birdwing butterfly (April 2024 - March 2025).

source.quote: The 'Dutchman's Pipe x Golden Birdwing Urban Habitat Creation Project' was launched in partnership with three schools.

metrics.context_qualifier: Wood chips produced from waste wood for mulch and soil enrichment over the year.

source.quote: in excess of 8 tonnes of wood chip

metrics.context_qualifier: Thousands of genetically diverse individuals of rare native plant species planted and cared for over the past 12 months.

source.quote: Over the past 12 months, we planted out and cared for thousands of genetically diverse individuals of rare native species
RESCUE RESULTS: 1 APRIL 2024-31 MARCH 2025

significant_events.summary: Received 3,329 wild animals for rescue and rehabilitation, including birds, mammals, reptiles, and snakes, during the reporting year.

source.quote: Kadoorie Conservation China (KCC) actively engages in ecosystem and biodiversity conservation across South China.

significant_events.summary: Kadoorie Conservation China (KCC) actively engaged in ecosystem and biodiversity conservation across South China, focusing on endangered species and community education.

source.quote: In collaboration with the Guangxi Bangliang Gibbon National Nature Reserve (BLNNR), KCC continued to implement community-engaged strategies to conserve the critically endangered Cao-Vit Gibbon (Nomascus nasutus).

significant_events.summary: Continued community-engaged strategies to conserve critically endangered Cao-Vit Gibbon, including sustainable livelihood initiatives and education camps.

significant_events.event_name: Eurasian Otter Conservation in South China

source.quote: KCC continues to work closely with various partners to conserve the regionally endangered Eurasian Otter (Lutra lutra)

significant_events.summary: Continued efforts to conserve regionally endangered Eurasian Otters, including rediscovery of signs and photographic evidence in Hong Kong.

source.quote: KCC launched a new conservation project aimed at monitoring the wild populations and promoting freshwater turtle conservation.

significant_events.summary: Launched a new project to monitor wild populations and promote freshwater turtle conservation, including micro-chipping and poaching identification.

source.quote: major components of the year's CLP-sponsored forest restoration project were executed across our upper slopes.

significant_events.summary: Executed major components of a CLP-sponsored forest restoration project, including weeding and tree works to create space for diverse native seedlings.

source.quote: From July to October 2024, more than 4,000 tree and shrub seedlings representing almost 200 native species... were then planted into an area of just under one ha.

significant_events.summary: Planted over 4,000 native tree and shrub seedlings of almost 200 species in a one-hectare area for forest restoration (July-October 2024).

source.quote: With support from the Hainan forestry authorities, KCC initiated an island-wide otter habitat research and assessment.

significant_events.summary: Initiated island-wide otter habitat research on Hainan Island, confirming Asian Small-Clawed Otters and identifying suitable habitats.

source.quote: KCC's study revealed that this invasive species was introduced to the territory through horticultural imports and we were able to identify the original source and potential loophole.

significant_events.summary: Study identified origin and pathway of invasive Blue-Crested Lizard, sharing findings with authorities to prevent further introductions.

source.quote: This year the vet and rehabilitation team managed to save three Spoonbills and see them released to join the wild population

significant_events.summary: Rescued and released three Black-Faced Spoonbills with suspected botulism during the reporting year.

source.quote: Members of the rescue team prepare to pack several Arakan Forest Turtles (seized by the authorities) that originate from western Myanmar. They were transferred to Turtle Island Centre, Austria in December 2024.

significant_events.summary: Rehomed several Arakan Forest Turtles seized from illegal trade to a conservation breeding program in Austria (December 2024).

source.quote: Over the past 12 months, we planted out and cared for thousands of genetically diverse individuals of rare native species

significant_events.summary: Planted and cared for thousands of genetically diverse individuals of rare native plant species over the past 12 months.

source.quote: education programmes for more than 40,000 children annually.

significant_events.summary: Provided education programmes to over 40,000 children annually, focusing on nature and sustainable lifestyles.

source.quote: Initiated in December 2023, this programme conducted 48 sessions and engaged 2,242 migrant domestic workers through March 2025.

significant_events.summary: Engaged 2,242 migrant domestic workers in 48 sessions to connect with nature, fostering well-being and community (December 2023 - March 2025).

source.quote: a school programme engaged primary and secondary students across four schools to deepen their understanding of snakes.

significant_events.summary: School program engaged students across four schools to deepen understanding of snakes through talks, visits, and creative projects.

source.quote: The 'Dutchman's Pipe x Golden Birdwing Urban Habitat Creation Project' was launched in partnership with three schools.

significant_events.summary: Partnered with three schools to teach students how to grow host plants for the Golden Birdwing butterfly and create habitats.

data.original_text: The Farm was established in 1956 to provide agricultural aid to farmers in need of support to help them lead independent lives; and to provide leisure and educational experience for the public.

The KAAA's aim was to encourage a philosophy of 'Helping People Help Themselves'. Those in need were given training, provided with agricultural inputs and interest-free loans to set them on the path to becoming independent.

In 1956, the KAAA established an experimental and extension farm at Paak Ngau Shek (the present site of KFBG) as a centre to demonstrate effective and profitable crop production and animal husbandry techniques, to improve livestock breeds and to train local farmers and Hong Kong-based Gurkha soldiers in farming so that they could have income when they returned home to Nepal.
Kadoorie Farm and Botanic Garden (KFBG), a Hong Kong based NGO, was established in 1956 to provide agricultural aid to farmers in need of support to help them lead independent lives.

Since the 1990s, reflecting the changing social needs, KFBG plays an active role in promoting biodiversity conservation in Hong Kong and South China, allied with organic agriculture and environmental education.
It also serves as a centre for wildlife rescue and rehabilitation, holistic education and sustainable agriculture, and receives on average 157,000 visitors each year.

Kadoorie Farm and Botanic Garden (KFBG) raises awareness, undertakes rigorous science-based species conservation and ecosystem restoration, and offers new ways of thinking and low-carbon living to respond to the world's environmental problems, all while being linked to the resources and lifestyle of Hong Kong.
Our work brings hope and improvement, by focusing on nature conservation, sustainable living and holistic education that reconnect people with nature.
By working together with the public, government, academia, NGOs and businesses, we help to protect our common future.
KFBG's three core programmes, focused on nature conservation, holistic education and sustainable living, are constantly evolving as they strive to harmonise humanity's relationship with nature.

In addition to the many on-site courses, workshops and special events we run, we implement projects throughout Hong Kong, Greater Bay Area and other parts of the Indo-Burma region to better protect and restore biodiversity.

Within KFBG, there are streams, woodlands, orchards, vegetable gardens, walking trails, separate exhibits focusing on live animals, flora and art, sustainable agriculture demonstration plots, a wild animal rescue centre, a native tree nursery, conservation and education facilities, and magical, misty fern gardens.
In addition, in April 2024, we opened the Kadoorie Centre - Food Hub, for residential retreats and innovative cooking.

Now it is managed to integrate nature conservation, including a rescue and rehabilitation programme for native animals, along with holistic education and practices in support of a transition to sustainable living.
The farm attracts about 3000 to 5000 visitors per week.
Programmes run by KFBG's Education Department include tree planting, improving wildlife habitat, art and environment workshops, as well as outreach programmes for schools and the local community.

KFBG has a range of biodiversity conservation programmes. Its Ecological Advisory Programme, launched in 1998, advises government, environmental NGOs, villagers, ecological consultants, academics and private developers, seeking to influence policy and practice in support of conservation.
The Fauna and Flora Conservation Departments contribute through wildlife rescue work, ex-situ breeding and propagation programmes, and educational projects.
KFBG seeks to integrate its multiple management objectives, for biodiversity, ecosystem services and holistic education, on its own 150-hectare estate.

Meanwhile, through its Sustainable Living & Agriculture Department KFBG works to support community transition by developing new and economically workable opportunities for all parties in the food system.

Our mission is to harmonise our relationship with the environment, and we play an active role in promoting nature conservation and sustainability in Hong Kong, Mainland China, Laos and Cambodia through fauna and flora conservation, wildlife rescue, nature education and sustainable agriculture.
We aim to continuously bring hope, happiness and improvement by working together with the public, government, academia, NGOs and businesses to help protect our common future.
We also offer a wide range of nature education programmes which give people the chance to reconnect and develop a relationship with nature.

Our Live education exhibits feature rescued native and exotic animal species unable to return to the wild, but with an important role to raise awareness among visitors and promote connections with nature.
To address important conservation needs in Hong Kong and the region, a Wild Animal Rescue Centre was developed along with projects focussed on endangered species conservation too.
In addition to the fauna conservation, KFBG conserves rare plants in the wild, cultivates native diversity and restores native forest.

Its core programmes—nature conservation, holistic education, and sustainable living—aim to reconnect people with nature through on-site courses, workshops, community events, and collaborative projects with government, academia, NGOs, and businesses.

Over time, as Hong Kong's economy shifted away from farming, KFBG evolved its focus to address environmental challenges, becoming a registered non-profit corporation in 1995 via ordinance to promote biodiversity conservation, ecosystem restoration, and low-carbon lifestyles in Hong Kong, the Greater Bay Area, and the Indo-Burma region.

The Kadoorie brothers, part of a well-established business family, saw wealth as a sacred trust to benefit others. Through such aid, thousands of people received agricultural training: thousands of pigs, chickens and ducks were bred and given to farmers or sold to them on credit as micro-loans and numerous wells, irrigation channels, roads, footpaths, bridges, pigsties and farmhouses were built.
As a regional leader in the field, our rescue centre have received and cared for over 40,000 wild animals.
Between 1994 and 2015, the Rescue Centre received over 36,000 animals.
The depth and breadth of our forest restoration project has been deemed by experts to be globally unique, with its standards now being officially applied internationally.
Our Green Hub for sustainable living has been widely acclaimed, winning a number of awards.

KCC was invited to lead the Hainan Gibbon conservation project since 2003, and has been implementing a multi-pronged conservation action plan to prevent the extinction of Hainan Gibbon. After 15 years of conservation effort, the population has doubled in number.

Kadoorie Farm and Botanic Garden's long-running, scientifically informed forest restoration programme has been awarded Premium-Tier Certification by The Global Biodiversity Standard, the first site in the world to achieve this accolade.
Over just eight years of informed growing and planting practices, a young secondary forest comprising exceptionally high diversity of native trees has emerged.
The K.A.A.A gave as gifts a total of 3,367 cattle to the villagers of Hong Kong.
The result was a revitalisation, not only of the local economy, but also of the hopes and dreams of the people of the New Territories and Outlying Islands.

KFBG's work is diverse; whereas the Wildlife Animal Rescue Centre received more than 4,000 animals, including 156 turtles from the illegal wildlife trade, the Flora Conservation Department further developed a model for habitation restoration with the native tree species of Hong Kong.
While the year started without visitors, from 1 April 2022 to 1 April 2023 we welcomed 150,783 people, of which 34,716 came in February alone.
In 2020, 16,849 people visited the Green Hub and 436 people attended the new courses that were held online.
